components.label: Search Client (Web/App)
components.description: Sends search query to Query API; receives ranked result list with snippets; autocomplete requests go to a separate low-latency suggest service

components.label: Query Processing Service + Crawler Fleet + Indexer
components.description: Query Service intersects postings lists across index shards and merges/re-ranks results; Crawler Fleet runs distributed BFS over the web graph; Indexer parses crawled pages and writes to the inverted index

components.label: Inverted Index (Sharded by Doc ID Range)
components.description: Maps terms to postings lists: {term → [(doc_id, tf_idf_score), ...]}; sharded horizontally by document ID range; each shard answers queries in parallel (scatter-gather pattern)

components.label: Query Result Cache (Redis)
components.description: Caches full result pages for the top 1% of queries (navigational queries like 'facebook', 'youtube') — these account for ~30% of all traffic; TTL varies by query freshness requirements

components.label: Document Store + Web Graph + PageRank Store
components.description: Document Store holds crawled page content and extracted text; Web Graph stores outbound link relationships for PageRank computation; PageRank Store holds pre-computed scores per doc_id

components.label: URL Frontier + Indexing Pipeline Queue (Kafka)
components.description: URL Frontier is a priority queue of URLs to crawl, ordered by estimated PageRank and freshness; Indexing Pipeline queue carries parsed page content from crawlers to indexers asynchronously

connections.label: Check result cache for popular queries
Scatter: fan-out query to all index shards in parallel
Gather: each shard returns top-k docs with scores
Fetch PageRank scores + snippet text for top results
Crawl discovery: push newly found URLs to URL frontier
Indexing pipeline: deliver parsed page content to indexer
Batch index write: new documents added to inverted index

keyInsights: The inverted index inverts the natural document→words relationship into words→documents: instead of asking 'what words does doc_1 contain?', it answers 'which documents contain the word system?' by looking up the postings list for 'system' — this is what makes sub-millisecond term lookups possible across 50 billion documents

Query processing as set intersection: for the query 'system design interview', retrieve the postings lists for all three terms and intersect them — only documents containing all three words are candidates; starting with the rarest term ('interview' has a shorter postings list than 'system') minimises the working set before intersection

Scatter-gather across shards: the index is horizontally sharded by document ID range (shard 1: doc IDs 0-1B, shard 2: doc IDs 1B-2B, etc.); a query fans out to all shards simultaneously, each returns its local top-k results, and a central merger re-ranks the combined set by global score — query latency is bounded by the slowest shard, not the total index size

PageRank is a batch computation: PageRank requires iterating over the full web graph (50B nodes, trillions of edges) — this takes hours on a distributed MapReduce cluster; the computed score per doc_id is stored and merged with real-time query signals (CTR, freshness, personalisation) at query time

Bloom filter for crawler deduplication: maintaining a hash set of 50 billion crawled URLs would require terabytes of memory; a Bloom filter represents the same 50B URLs in ~60GB (10 bits/element at 1% false-positive rate) and answers 'have I seen this URL?' in O(1) with zero false negatives — occasionally a new URL is mistakenly flagged as seen (false positive), but a URL is never missed

tradeoffs.decision: Horizontal index sharding by doc ID over vertical sharding by term range
tradeoffs.pro: Even data distribution; all shards participate in every query enabling full parallelism; new documents distributed across all shards
tradeoffs.con: Every query must fan out to all shards; cannot isolate hot terms (e.g. 'the', 'a') to dedicated high-memory shards

tradeoffs.decision: Pre-computed PageRank over real-time link analysis
tradeoffs.pro: PageRank computation over 50B nodes is impractical at query time; offline batch gives accurate scores without impacting query latency
tradeoffs.con: PageRank scores are hours to days stale; newly published highly-linked pages appear lower in results until the next PageRank computation

tradeoffs.decision: Separate crawl pipeline and indexing pipeline
tradeoffs.pro: Decouples crawl throughput (network-bound) from indexing throughput (CPU-bound); each scales independently; failures in one do not block the other
tradeoffs.con: Adds end-to-end latency from page discovery to index availability; a newly published page may not appear in search results for hours

antiPatterns: Full-text LIKE queries on a relational database — SELECT * FROM documents WHERE content LIKE '%system design%' is a full table scan; completely impractical at 50B documents
Single-node inverted index — no single machine has enough RAM to hold an index for 50B documents; the index must be sharded and queries must fan out across shards
Crawling every URL on every crawl cycle — re-crawling stable pages (e.g. historical Wikipedia articles) wastes resources; the URL frontier must prioritise high-PageRank and recently-updated pages
Computing PageRank at query time — even for a small subset of the web graph, PageRank requires multiple graph traversal iterations; it must be pre-computed offline and stored as a score per doc_id
